text: --- ## Top Stories ### The US Military Buildup Against Iran: Largest Since 2003 The Trump administration has assembled what military analysts are now describing as the most concentrated display of US military force in the Middle East since the 2003 invasion of Iraq. Four American carrier strike groups are either in the region or converging on it: the USS Gerald R. Ford arrived at Souda Bay on the island of Crete on Monday and is expected off the coast of Israel within days; the USS Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group is operating in the Arabian Sea. More than 40,000 US military personnel are now stationed across bases and naval assets in the region. Robert Pape, professor of political science at the University of Chicago and a leading analyst of American air power, wrote Saturday: "This represents 40-50% of the deployable US air power in the world. Think air power on the order of the 1991 and 2003 Iraq war. And growing. Never has the US deployed this much force against a potential enemy and not launched strikes." Col. Richard Kemp, former commander of British forces in Afghanistan, told the Media Line that the current scale already exceeds the June 2025 "12-day war" (Operation Midnight Hammer) and "could run into weeks." The State Department on Monday ordered the evacuation of non-essential personnel and family members from the US Embassy in Beirut — 32 to 50 staff and family members flew out of Beirut airport. The State Department updated its travel advisory for Lebanon, warning US citizens not to travel to the country. The WSWS's Andre Damon noted that this evacuation follows the identical pattern established before Operation Midnight Hammer in June 2025, when US presence was similarly reduced at embassies in Baghdad, Kuwait, and Bahrain in the days before B-2 bombers struck Iran's nuclear facilities. The Washington Post reported separately that the Joint Chiefs of Staff Chairman, Gen. Dan Caine, has privately warned the president that a US attack on Iran carries "acute risks" — including a lack of munitions and allied support — that differ sharply from Trump's public boasting that Caine had told him an attack would be an "easy victory." This contradiction between Trump's public claims and military reality is itself a dangerous indicator: it suggests the president is building a public justification for war that has been deliberately detached from the military's own risk assessments. From the WSWS's perspective, the bourgeois media's framing — the Financial Times headline "Crisis of his own making" is typical — obscures the bipartisan character of the war drive. The Democrats' objections to Trump on Iran are tactical, not principled. The NYT analysis noting that Tehran views concessions on uranium enrichment as riskier than war should be understood not as Iranian intransigence but as the rational response of a state that has watched how the United States treated Libya and Iraq after they gave up their weapons programs. The fourth anniversary of Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine (February 24, 2022) falls today. Four years in, the UN reports more than 15,000 civilians killed, including 763 children. Russia holds approximately 20 percent of Ukrainian territory, and front lines are largely frozen. Ukraine's Zelenskyy told the FT that the war is at "the beginning of the end" — but what that end looks like remains contested. Negotiations are now under way, with the NYT reporting that a major sticking point is the fate of Ukrainian citizens in Russian-controlled Donetsk, who face an "agonizing choice" about their political future. Hungary is blocking both the latest EU sanctions package on Russia and a $106 billion financial aid package to Kyiv, a pressure point that significantly weakens the Western position going into any talks. The BBC published a striking report today: four Russian soldiers testify that they witnessed fellow troops executed by commanders for refusing orders. This is not simply a human interest story. It is evidence of the coercive, non-voluntary character of Russia's own war effort — a point that connects to the WSWS's analysis of the war as driven by competing ruling class interests, with the working class on both sides bearing the catastrophic costs. ### Trump's State of the Union: Reeling from Supreme Court, Falling Polls Tonight's State of the Union address is being delivered under conditions the White House itself acknowledges are difficult. The NYT reports Trump is "bruised and unpopular," reeling from the 6-3 Supreme Court decision last Friday invalidating his "reciprocal tariffs" under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). His approval ratings are declining. The paper notes that "cracks appear in Trump coalition" in bellwether regions like Northeastern Pennsylvania, where working-class discontent is growing. The circumstances around tonight's speech are significant beyond the speech itself. The WSWS Perspectives published today — "The real state of Trump's America" — provides the counter-narrative that the speech will attempt to bury. Key facts that Trump will not mention: the national debt stands at $38.4 trillion ($113,000 per person), growing at $8 billion per day; cumulative price increases since 2020 include food up 25%, housing still climbing 3% annually; the top 1% control 31.7% of all wealth — the highest share since the Fed began tracking in 1989; Elon Musk's fortune has ballooned to $726 billion; CEO pay is 285 times average worker pay; 2025 was the deadliest year for ICE detention on record, with 66,000 people currently held. The Democrats have selected Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger — a CIA veteran — to deliver the party's response, just as they chose Senator Elissa Slotkin (also a CIA veteran) last year. The WSWS Perspectives correctly identifies this as a deliberate signal to the ruling class that the Democratic Party will uphold the military-intelligence apparatus regardless of the electoral outcome. Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez will offer their customary "left"-sounding rhetoric; in practice, the Democrats collaborate with Trump on the core class interests of the financial oligarchy. The "Angel Families" staging at the White House on Monday — families of people killed by undocumented immigrants — is the latest in Trump's cynical exploitation of victims to normalize the immigration crackdown. The NYT notes that public support for Trump's immigration approach is "softening." Hanging over the proceedings is the Jeffrey Epstein scandal. The WSWS Editorial Board's Perspectives makes this explicit: "The Justice Department's release of nearly 3 million pages has shaken the upper tiers of corporate America." Billionaire Leslie Wexner was identified as a co-conspirator in 2019 FBI documents yet faces no charges. The arrest in London of former UK Ambassador to the US Peter Mandelson on suspicion of "misconduct in public office" — tied to Epstein revelations — is a separate but connected story that reveals how far the rot extends into the transatlantic ruling class. ### Supreme Court Strikes Down Trump Tariffs — Trump Escalates Immediately Last Friday's 6-3 Supreme Court ruling in Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump struck down the "reciprocal tariffs" Trump had imposed under the IEEPA — a ruling that voided approximately $160 billion in tariffs collected over the past year. The decision was written by Chief Justice John Roberts, with Justices Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett joining the three liberals in the majority. Trump's response was immediate and revealing. At a hastily convened press conference, he attacked the three conservative justices who voted against him as "fools and lapdogs of the radical left," called the entire majority "major sleazebags" and "foreign country-centric," and compared himself to a Mafia don warning foreign countries that they would stop "dancing in the streets." The Wall Street Journal editorialized that "Trump owes the Supreme Court an apology." But the more significant story is what Trump did immediately after the ruling. He invoked Section 122 of the 1974 Trade Act — a provision never previously used at this scale by any president — to impose a 10-15% tariff on all imported goods for 150 days, after which Congressional approval is required for extension. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent described this as temporary. In the meantime, Trump has directed his legal team to pursue longer-term tariff authority under Section 301 of the same act. The Guardian is covering a related development: the 10% tariffs that came into effect Tuesday — the "replacement" tariffs following the Supreme Court ruling — are being greeted with anger by trading partners globally. China has simultaneously imposed export restrictions on 20 Japanese companies with defense ties, escalating the US-China confrontation in a different theater. The WSWS Perspectives by John Burton correctly identifies the Supreme Court ruling not as a restoration of constitutional norms but as an expression of deepening divisions within the ruling class over the strategic direction of US capitalism. Trump's willingness to attack three of his own appointees exposes how far the presidency has moved from any conventional understanding of institutional loyalty. ### Mexico: El Mencho Killed — US Intelligence Decisive — Violence Erupts On Sunday, Mexican security forces, with decisive assistance from US Northern Command intelligence, killed Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes — "El Mencho" — leader of the Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG), in a compound in Tapalpa, Jalisco. El Mencho was tracked after surveillance of a romantic partner he traveled to visit. Defense Secretary Ricardo Trevilla acknowledged that "the exchange of information and data" with US Northern Command was "how we arrived at this specific case." The operation killed 25 soldiers, 30 alleged CJNG members, and 3 civilians, including a pregnant woman. 70 arrests were made across seven states. The Mexican government immediately deployed 10,000 additional troops nationwide. In the days since, violence has erupted across western Mexico: schools shut in several states, businesses closed, US citizens were warned to shelter in place in Puerto Vallarta and Cancún. Guadalajara — a 2026 World Cup host city — descended into chaos. The WSWS's Andrea Lobo provides the essential class analysis: the "war on drugs" is not a humanitarian project but a mechanism for deepening US imperialist control over Mexico and justifying the militarization of the country's internal security apparatus — directed ultimately against the Mexican working class. The killing of El Mencho does not end the cartel; it will likely trigger a violent succession struggle within CJNG and provoke inter-cartel warfare, which will in turn be used as a pretext for expanded military deployment. The broader context is the Trump administration's ongoing coercion of the Mexican government. Trump has continued to demand that Mexico "step up" its anti-cartel efforts even after this major operation, signaling that no level of compliance will satisfy Washington's demands. The CIA's role (reported by WaPo) in tracking El Mencho further underscores that this is a US intelligence operation conducted on Mexican soil with the acquiescence of a government under severe imperial pressure. ### Kaiser Strike and NewYork-Presbyterian Nurses: Twin Betrayals in One Day Two major healthcare worker strikes were brought to anticlimactic ends within the past 48 hours, exposing the systematic function of the union bureaucracy as a mechanism for demobilizing workers rather than representing them. **Kaiser Permanente:** On Monday, UNAC/UHCP officials ordered an abrupt end to the four-week strike of 31,000 healthcare workers — nurses, pharmacists, and clinicians in California and Hawaii. Workers were told to return by 7:00 AM Tuesday. No contract was ratified. No membership vote was held. The union described "significant movement at the bargaining table" without disclosing any specifics. By calling the action "suspended" rather than ending it through ratification, the leadership bypassed democratic procedures entirely and used its own discretionary authority to demobilize the strike. The WSWS's Marc Wells correctly identifies this as "a flagrant violation of members' basic democratic rights." **NewYork-Presbyterian:** On Saturday, NYSNA officials announced the ratification of a contract ending a six-week strike by 4,000 nurses — the last of four facilities still on strike. The key facts: nurses had overwhelmingly rejected the previous tentative agreement just over a week before; the new ratification vote was conducted hours after the deal was announced, with no time for workers to read or discuss the contract; the vote was conducted online under conditions that NYSNA itself had already acknowledged as insecure (the previous vote was on SurveyMonkey); and the union had isolated nurses in below-freezing temperatures while withholding strike pay to coerce ratification. The contract does not adequately address safe staffing. These two betrayals on the same day, combined with the WGA West staff strike — in which 115 administrative workers are striking against their own union — reveal the trade union apparatus as an instrument of class collaboration, not class struggle. The developing strike wave is real; the question is whether workers can build the rank-and-file organizations necessary to wage it independently of the bureaucracy. --- ## International Developments ### Ukraine War: Year Five — Stalemate, Atrocities, and Deteriorating Western Position The fourth anniversary of Russia's February 24, 2022 full-scale invasion brings no resolution. The NYT reports that Ukraine faces an "agonizing choice" over the fate of residents in Russian-controlled Donetsk — people who have lived under occupation and may face a peace deal that leaves them formally under Russian sovereignty. The BBC published testimony from four Russian soldiers claiming they witnessed their own comrades executed for refusing to advance. Russia's war economy is increasingly strained. A major NYT analysis reveals that approximately half of Russia's federal budget now goes to the Ukraine war — spending that "does little to support its long-term development" while enriching defense contractors and a narrow military-industrial stratum. The ruble has been partially stabilized through capital controls and commodity exports, but Russia's long-term productive capacity is being hollowed out. Meanwhile, the Western coalition supporting Ukraine is fracturing. Hungary — under the Orban government — is blocking both the latest EU sanctions package against Russia and a €96 billion ($106 billion) financial aid package to Kyiv, according to the NYT. This gives Russia a diplomatic opening and significantly weakens the position of the NATO bloc going into any ceasefire talks. The EU's ability to sustain Ukraine economically is a political, not just a financial, question. Ukraine's President Zelenskyy told the Financial Times that the war is "at the beginning of the end" — a formulation that is diplomatically significant because it signals Kyiv's openness to negotiations while trying to manage expectations. The actual negotiating positions remain far apart: Russia's demands for territorial concessions and Ukrainian neutrality remain unacceptable to Kyiv, and the social base for a compromise peace in Ukraine itself is narrow, as the NYT's reporting from Donetsk makes clear. ### France: Fascist Mobilization and the Macron Government's Complicity The death of Quentin Deranque — a young supporter of the far-right organization Lyon Populaire who died following a confrontation after a lecture by France Unbowed's Palestinian-origin Euro-MP Rima Hassan — has been seized upon by the entire French political establishment to launch a coordinated offensive against the left. The WSWS's Anthony Torres identifies the key political fact: a political front spanning fascist groups, the Socialist Party, the National Rally (Marine Le Pen and Jordan Bardella), and President Macron's own government has formed around the Deranque death to criminalize left-wing political activity. Macron explicitly blessed the far-right demonstration in Deranque's honor at the opening of the Agricultural Fair. The French government has refused to ban the fascist mobilization while simultaneously using the event to attack France Unbowed and the broader left. The WSWS draws the parallel with the United States, where the death of fascist agitator Charlie Kirk was similarly exploited to mobilize the right and delegitimize opposition. The objective in France, as Torres writes, is to clear the way for either Le Pen or Bardella to assume power after the forthcoming local elections and 2027 presidential election. The US ambassador to France, Charles Kushner (son of Jared Kushner's father), further inflamed the situation by citing "violent radical leftism" in the Deranque death — a formulation the French government found unacceptable and formally protested, summoning Kushner twice. He declined to appear on both occasions, sending a junior official in his place. ### Australia: Democratic Rights Under Labor Attack on Multiple Fronts The Albanese Labor government's assault on democratic rights is accelerating on several fronts simultaneously, all of which the WSWS's Australian correspondents are documenting: **The Bondi terrorist attack cover-up:** More than two months after the December 14 attack in which IS-linked father-son pair Sajid and Naveed Akram killed 15 people at a Jewish gathering at Bondi Beach, Oscar Grenfell reports that the central political question — how known-to-ASIO perpetrators were able to plan, arm, and execute the attack without interception — remains entirely unanswered. The attack is being invoked to justify sweeping new powers (protest bans, political party proscription) without any scrutiny of the intelligence and security failures that allowed it to occur. **Banning Hizb-ut Tahrir:** Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke has initiated the banning of Hizb-ut Tahrir under new legislation that does not require any terrorism link — only political speech deemed to promote "hate speech." Burke explicitly boasted that this is "the first time we have been able to ban a group which falls short of a terrorist listing." The legislation, as the WSWS has previously reported, is structured in a way that could be used to ban political parties. **Police raid on anti-fascist artwork:** On February 18, three ACT Policing officers entered the Dissent Cafe and Bar in Canberra after an anonymous Crime Stoppers complaint about satirical posters depicting Trump, Musk, Netanyahu, Vance, and Putin in Nazi-style uniforms, produced by British protest collective Grow Up Art. When the owner refused to surrender the posters, police declared the premises a crime scene for more than two hours. Detectives from the Major Crimes Unit were later dispatched to seize the posters. The action is being treated as a test case under the Combatting Antisemitism, Hate and Extremism Act 2026. **Healthcare and education strikes betrayed:** The ANMFSA (South Australian nurses' union) suspended all industrial action after a backroom deal with the Labor state government that fell far short of workers' 21% pay demand. In Victoria, early childhood educators are striking Thursday — but the AEU is keeping them separated from the March 24 school teacher stoppage to prevent unified action against the Labor government. ### Israel-Palestine: Settlers, Annexation, and the Berlinale Protests Twenty countries issued a joint statement condemning Israel's "de facto annexation" drive in the occupied West Bank, calling it a "deliberate and direct attack on the viability of a Palestinian state." The statement represents a significant diplomatic development but carries no enforcement mechanism. The NYT published a detailed report by Azam Ahmed on the systematic dispossession of Palestinian families in the West Bank through settler violence. The Rezeq Abu Naim family, after two years of continuous attacks on their land at all hours and in all forms, finally abandoned their home after another violent incursion over the weekend. This is not exceptional; it is the operational method of what twenty countries are now officially calling annexation. At the Berlin International Film Festival (Berlinale), award-winning filmmakers used the closing ceremony to denounce German government complicity in Gaza genocide. Stefan Steinberg's WSWS report describes how festival management — including director Tricia Tuttle and jury head Wim Wenders — deliberately sought to prevent any political discussion of Palestine, with Wenders initially declaring artists should "stay out of politics." The protest from filmmakers was so sustained and forceful that it dominated the closing ceremony despite these attempts at suppression. The political character of the German cultural establishment's silence on genocide is itself a story. ### UK: Mandelson Arrest, Epstein Fallout, and the Crisis of Starmerism The arrest on Monday of Peter Mandelson — former UK Ambassador to the United States, key architect of Blairite "New Labour," and longtime central figure in the Labour establishment — on suspicion of "misconduct in public office" has sent shockwaves through British politics. The arrest followed revelations about Mandelson's dealings with Jeffrey Epstein. This is the deepest penetration of the Epstein scandal into the British political establishment since the arrest of Prince Andrew earlier this month. The NYT reports that the Mandelson arrest "deepens a scandal that has led to calls for Prime Minister Keir Starmer's resignation." Starmer's Labour government, which came to power in 2024 promising a break from the corruption of the Conservative years, is now implicated through its most senior figures in the Epstein network's exposure. The political implications extend beyond Labour's domestic difficulties. Mandelson is a figure who has operated at the intersection of transatlantic ruling class networks for decades. His arrest, combined with the UK's move to back Australia on removing Prince Andrew from the line of succession, suggests that the Epstein exposure is beginning to consume figures previously considered untouchable. ### China-Japan Economic Confrontation Escalates China placed export restrictions on 20 Japanese companies with defense industry ties — the latest escalation in an ongoing economic confrontation between Beijing and Tokyo that has developed alongside Japan's rearmament drive. The NYT frames this as a "monthslong feud." The broader context is China's role in the global economic restructuring driven by the end of the post-Cold War liberal order: Beijing is now explicitly using export controls on critical materials (rare earths, semiconductor components) as a geopolitical weapon in the same way the US uses financial sanctions. The WSWS has not yet covered this specific development. It connects directly to: the Taiwan semiconductor scenario (NYT published a major analysis today of the vulnerability of US tech industry if China cuts off Taiwan chip exports); the broader US-China military confrontation; and the Japanese government's decision to dramatically increase defense spending under US pressure. --- ## United States ### Georgia Concentration Camp: 10,000-Bed Detention Complex The Trump administration's plans for a 10,000-bed immigrant detention complex in Social Circle, Georgia — detailed in Jacob Crosse's WSWS report — represent the largest planned civilian mass detention facility since the Japanese American internment of World War II. Utility filings project more than one million gallons per day in wastewater demand, consistent with permanent large-scale operation. The broader development plan encompasses more than 2.3 million square feet. This is not a "surge" measure. It is infrastructure for indefinite mass incarceration on a scale that would make the United States the world's leading incarcerator of a targeted ethnic and political group. The plan is provoking outrage — which the bourgeois press covers — but without the class analysis: this concentration camp network is not simply an immigration enforcement tool. It is, as the WSWS Perspectives notes, the infrastructure of a police state directed ultimately against the entire working class, beginning with its most vulnerable sector. Congress has already allocated $45 billion for detention expansion, with projected capacity of 135,000 — double the current record level of 66,000. ### Quakertown, Pennsylvania: Students Jailed, Police Cover-Up At least five high school students from Quakertown Community High School in Bucks County, Pennsylvania remain in police custody more than 72 hours after being arrested during an anti-ICE walkout on Friday, February 20. The incident began when Police Chief Scott McElree — not in uniform — physically grabbed students during the protest, with video widely circulated showing him choking a young female student. Another officer was filmed throwing a student into a planter. Five students and one adult were arrested. As of this writing, no charging documents have been released, no body camera footage has been shared, and the department has issued no public statement explaining McElree's use of force. A Change.org petition demanding McElree's resignation has exceeded 7,800 signatures; a GoFundMe for legal and medical costs has raised nearly $33,000. The IYSSE statement published today correctly frames this not as an isolated incident of police misconduct but as part of a "broader campaign to intimidate youth, criminalize political opposition and normalize police-state methods." Student walkouts against ICE have now occurred in more than 36 states. Will Lehman, the Mack Trucks worker and UAW presidential candidate, issued a statement calling the Quakertown assault "police brutality against children" and demanding working class solidarity with the students. The 323-student suspension at Woodbridge Senior High School in Prince William County, Virginia — for participating in an anti-ICE walkout — is documented in the IYSSE statement. Administrative punishment is being used alongside police violence as a two-pronged strategy to suppress the youth movement. ### Pentagon, Iran, and the Military-Industrial Budget The Pentagon is racing to spend $153 billion in added funds — money Republicans pushed through in their "sweeping domestic policy bill" — while having ignored Congress's demands for a spending plan for months. This is the militarization of the US economy in real time, directed toward war preparations rather than social needs. The Anthropic-Pentagon confrontation is a significant development: the Defense Department summoned Anthropic's CEO after the AI company demanded guardrails be maintained in a major defense contract. This reveals how AI is being conscripted into the military apparatus, and the pressure being placed on tech companies to remove safety restrictions for military applications. The Pentagon separately pulled its support for an air safety bill — legislation written in response to the January 2025 D.C. helicopter-plane collision — just as the House was expected to vote on it. This means the safety improvements prompted by a disaster that killed dozens of people are being blocked by the military. ### The Epstein Files and the Decomposition of the American Ruling Class The WSWS Perspectives today weaves the Epstein scandal into its analysis of the State of the Union — and rightly so. The Justice Department's release of nearly 3 million pages has now resulted in: Attorney General Pam Bondi deflecting questions by citing the Dow Jones index; FBI documents showing billionaire Leslie Wexner identified as a co-conspirator in 2019 with no charges brought; and email evidence of Epstein's own clinical dehumanization of the girls he trafficked. Trump himself is deeply implicated. The British dimension — Mandelson's arrest, Andrew's earlier arrest — reveals that this is not an American aberration but a feature of the transatlantic ruling class as a social formation. David North's characterization, cited in the Perspectives, of the Epstein revelations as revealing "the social physiognomy of a degenerate ruling class and oligarchical society in an advanced state of decomposition" is the correct political-historical framework for understanding what the files expose: not the crimes of bad individuals, but the systematic character of a social class that has detached itself from any restraining legal or moral framework. --- ## Science and Public Health ### Bhattacharya Controls NIH and CDC — Measles Surges — Public Health Infrastructure Being Demolished The Trump administration confirmed last week that NIH Director Jay Bhattacharya — best known as the Stanford health economist who co-authored the Great Barrington Declaration advocating for COVID-19 "herd immunity" — will simultaneously serve as acting director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Benjamin Mateus's WSWS analysis is essential reading. Bhattacharya has no formal training in public health administration and no experience directing infectious disease response. His "primary qualification," as Mateus correctly writes, "stems from his role during the COVID-19 pandemic as a leading propagandist for the mass infection policies demanded by Wall Street." The Great Barrington Declaration was written at the American Institute for Economic Research, a libertarian think tank tied to Charles Koch, and advocated "herd immunity" — a position that the WSWS's Global Workers' Inquest into the COVID-19 Pandemic has extensively documented as a deliberate policy of mass death in the service of capitalist profit. Now the same person controls both the NIH — the US government's primary biomedical research agency — and the CDC — its leading disease surveillance institution. This is occurring while measles cases are surging across the United States: a preventable disease returning in the context of RFK Jr.'s anti-vaccination campaign (Kennedy last week defended Trump's order ramping up glyphosate production before angry "MAHA Moms" who had not anticipated he would take that position on pesticides). The dismantling of public health is not a policy failure; it is a policy success from the standpoint of the financial interests who want to remove regulatory obstacles to profit. The Supreme Court's agreement to consider whether states can sue fossil fuel companies over greenhouse gas emissions is a separate development but part of the same pattern: the systematic weakening of regulatory constraints on capital. Dozens of municipalities are seeking billions in damages; a positive ruling for plaintiffs could have significant financial implications for the oil and gas industry. --- ## WSWS Coverage — Last 24 Hours ### "The real state of Trump's America: Social misery, dictatorship, war — and an upsurge of class struggle" — by WSWS Editorial Board **[FULL TEXT — Perspectives article published February 24, 2026]** US President Donald Trump will deliver the annual State of the Union address Tuesday night before a joint session of Congress. Were it honestly titled, the speech would be called "The State of the Rich"—a demagogic exercise in self-congratulation, burying the catastrophic reality of American life beneath a mountain of lies. The speech is being delivered in the year that the United States is to celebrate the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence. On July 4, 2026, the nation will commemorate the issuing of the document that proclaimed "all men are created equal" and asserted the inalienable rights of "Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness." The founders fought a revolution against King George III. Two hundred and fifty years later, Trump's America offers its own answer to the Declaration's ideals: a government of the billionaires, by the billionaires and for the billionaires, presiding over armed raids on immigrant communities, a network of concentration camps spreading across the country, children torn from their parents, and the gunning down of unarmed civilians by federal agents on the streets of Minneapolis. Hanging over the proceedings is the stench of Jeffrey Epstein. The Justice Department's release of nearly 3 million pages has shaken the upper tiers of corporate America, exposing connections of billionaires, chief executives and senior political figures to a convicted child sex trafficker. The files, as WSWS Chairman David North recently wrote, "reveal the social physiognomy of a degenerate ruling class and oligarchical society in an advanced state of decomposition. Their offenses are rank; they smell to heaven." Trump himself is deeply implicated. Attorney General Pam Bondi responded to questions about the ongoing cover-up by boasting that the Dow had hit 50,000. FBI documents reveal that billionaire Leslie Wexner was identified as a co-conspirator in 2019, yet no charges have been brought. Emails show Epstein comparing the girls he trafficked to "shrimp—you throw away the head and keep the body." This is the ruling class that governs America. Consider the facts that will be buried beneath Trump's demagogy. The national debt stands at $38.4 trillion, or $113,000 for every person in the country, and is increasing at $8 billion per day. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) projects the deficit at $1.9 trillion this year, growing to $3.1 trillion by 2036, with debt reaching 120 percent of GDP, which is higher than at any point in the nation's history. The dollar fell more than 9 percent in 2025, its worst annual performance since 2017, and remains in bear market territory as BRICS nations increase local currency trade settlements from 35 to 50 percent. Cumulative price increases since 2020 have been devastating: food up more than 25 percent, housing costs still climbing at 3 percent annually, natural gas up nearly 10 percent. The level of social inequality is historically unprecedented. The top 1 percent of households now control 31.7 percent of all wealth, the highest share since the Fed began tracking such data in 1989. They hold $55 trillion, nearly as much as the bottom 90 percent combined. The combined wealth of 935 American billionaires surged to $8.1 trillion at the end of 2025. Elon Musk, Trump's partner in dismantling the federal government through DOGE, has seen his fortune balloon to $726 billion. CEO compensation at S&P 500 companies averaged $18.9 million—285 times the typical worker's pay, up from 21-to-1 in 1965. CEO pay has risen 1,094 percent since 1978, while worker pay has increased just 26 percent. Tesla has proposed a $1 trillion pay package for Musk, exceeding the total annual compensation of the entire federal workforce. The stock market is not a measure of national prosperity. It is a barometer of ruling class enrichment. But the most sinister dimension of the real state of the union is the deployment of armed federal agents against the population. In December 2025, the administration launched Operation Metro Surge, deploying 3,000 masked ICE and Border Patrol agents into the Twin Cities, Minnesota, in what the DHS itself called the largest immigration operation in American history. On January 7, ICE shot and killed Renée Good, a 37-year-old American citizen and mother of three. On January 24, Border Patrol agents shot and killed Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old ICU nurse, firing as many as 10 rounds into him as he lay on the ground. The administration has built a network of detention camps across the country, holding nearly 66,000 people—a 75 percent increase, the highest level in history. Congress has allocated $45 billion for still more facilities, with capacity projected at 135,000. Children are separated from their parents at a record rate. A tent camp at Fort Bliss, Texas, holds 5,000 people on the same ground that served as an internment camp for Japanese Americans during World War II. 2025 was the deadliest year for ICE detention on record. The Democrats will offer no genuine opposition. Whatever gestures they make Tuesday night will be meaningless stunts. In actual practice, they collaborate with Trump on the issues that matter most to the ruling class. Where their differences with Trump exist, they are centered on tactical questions of imperialist strategy—above all, dissatisfaction with his insufficient support for the US-NATO proxy war against Russia in Ukraine. But on the drive toward war with Iran, the two parties are in essential agreement. The Democrats' selection of CIA veterans—Senator Elissa Slotkin last year, Virginia Governor Abigail Spanberger this year—to deliver the party's response to Trump's State of the Union is itself an assurance to the ruling class that they will uphold the military-intelligence apparatus while using "left"-sounding demagogy from Bernie Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez to block the emergence of a mass movement from below. This is the real state of Trump's America in the 250th year since the Declaration of Independence: a society riven by class antagonism on a scale not seen since the Gilded Age, ruled by a criminal oligarchy that has dispensed with even the pretense of democratic governance, lurching toward war abroad and dictatorship at home. But there is another side to the equation, and it is the decisive one. The same crisis driving the ruling class toward fascism and war is propelling the working class into struggle. A developing strike wave, the mass anti-ICE protests, last year's "No Kings" marches, the ongoing walkouts by high school students—these are the initial expressions of a social force that neither party of big business can contain. The critical question is one of political program, perspective and organization. The opposition to the Trump administration cannot be entrusted to the Democrats, who are complicit in everything he does. It must be based on the independent mobilization of the working class. Workers must form rank-and-file committees in every workplace, school and neighborhood to coordinate resistance, demand an end to the ICE terror, defend immigrants, and prepare for a general strike against the government's program of war, austerity and dictatorship. The building of such committees, organized on the basis of a socialist and internationalist program, is the only realistic strategy for defeating the fascistic conspiracy of the American oligarchy. --- ### "US begins pulling staff from Beirut embassy amid Iran attack buildup" — by Andre Damon Published February 24, 2026. Damon reports the State Department ordered 32-50 non-essential staff and family members evacuated from the US Embassy in Beirut, updated its Lebanon travel advisory to "do not travel," and imposed travel restrictions on remaining personnel. This follows the identical pre-strike pattern established before Operation Midnight Hammer in June 2025. The USS Gerald R. --- ## Pseudo-left press review - [Left Voice (PTS/FT)](#left-voice-ptsft) — covers Berlinale and ICE resistance; frames political questions as matters of individual moral courage rather than class analysis; promotes local reform campaigns as substitutes for independent working-class politics - [Liberation News / PSL](#liberation-news--psl) — demands freedom for CPM-K leader Omole and comments on Epstein; calls for socialism without any strategy for achieving it; treats allied Stalinist parties as genuinely Marxist - [Socialist Alternative (ISA/CWI)](#socialist-alternative-isacwi) — issues general strike call for May 1 without addressing the union bureaucracies that would suppress it; frames Trump as "Trumpism in crisis" rather than fascist consolidation of state power - [SWP UK (Socialist Worker)](#swp-uk-socialist-worker) — celebrates Green by-election win as "anti-racist victory" using identity-politics framing that avoids the class character of the Green Party; reports on zero-hours contracts without a program beyond pressuring Labour - [Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT)](#socialist-appeal--rcp-imt) — focuses on British parliamentary maneuvers and self-promotional recruitment reports; no coverage of workers' struggles in this period ### Left Voice (PTS/FT) Left Voice published three notable pieces. On the **Berlinale closing ceremony**, Nathaniel Flakin correctly identified the significance of Palestinian director Abdallah Alkhatib's "partner in genocide" speech and the cowardice of Wenders and Paul Thomas Anderson in refusing to comment on Gaza. However, the article frames the question entirely in terms of individual moral courage versus cowardice — it asks why these artists will not speak up, rather than analyzing the class character of bourgeois film culture or the role of the German social-democratic state in enabling genocide. The structural complicity of European cultural institutions with imperialism goes unexamined. On **ICE in Metro Detroit**, Left Voice calls for a local campaign to "kick ICE out" of the area, "building on the lessons of Minneapolis." The article proposes a coalition and pressure campaign but does not challenge the Democratic Party apparatus that administers Detroit, nor does it offer an independent class strategy. Similarly, coverage of **UAW Local 2325's strike vote against ICE** presents a single local's resolution as a model for the labor movement — without addressing the UAW International leadership's role in systematically suppressing rank-and-file opposition, which is the central focus of the WSWS's coverage through Will Lehman's campaign. Left Voice's overall orientation: correct identification of significant political moments (Gaza, ICE raids) combined with a consistent refusal to draw class conclusions or challenge the political frameworks (Democratic Party, union bureaucracy) that contain working-class opposition.
Sources Left Voice Berlinale Film Festival: 'I'm not a politician' · Left Voice We need a campaign to kick ICE out of Metro Detroit · Left Voice UAW Local 2325 commits to a strike vote to fight ICE
### Liberation News / PSL The PSL issued a **statement demanding the release of Booker Ngesa Omole**, leader of the Communist Party Marxist-Kenya (CPM-K), jailed on what the PSL characterizes as fraudulent charges. The statement calls for international solidarity — a correct impulse — but characterizes Omole's party as "Marxist" without examining the CPM-K's actual political program or its relationship to Kenyan nationalism. Notably, the WSWS also covered Omole's detention this week, providing an area of convergent coverage. On the **Epstein file revelations**, PSL published "Justice will not be served for Epstein survivors under capitalism," which argues that the capitalist system makes genuine accountability impossible. The conclusion is superficially correct but operates at the level of social-democratic critique of wealth and corruption rather than Marxist analysis. There is no discussion of the specific connections exposed between Epstein, the Israeli state, US intelligence agencies, and the ruling class — precisely the concrete content that would give the abstract "capitalism" critique material force. The article offers no strategy for how workers can achieve the socialist transformation it vaguely invokes. PSL's overall orientation: solidarity statements and systemic critiques that stop at the threshold of concrete political analysis, particularly regarding the state and its intelligence apparatus.
Sources Liberation News PSL statement: Free Booker Ngesa Omole! · Liberation News Justice will not be served for Epstein survivors under capitalism
### Socialist Alternative (ISA/CWI) Socialist Alternative published a **State of the Union analysis** characterizing Trump as promoting "Trumpism in crisis" following the Supreme Court tariff ruling. The article calls for resistance "in the streets" and on May 1 but frames Trump's political program as a crisis of a personal tendency ("Trumpism") rather than the fascist consolidation of capitalist state power. There is no analysis of the Democrats' role in enabling or colluding with Trump's program. More significantly, SAlt published **"The Case for a Nationwide General Strike on May 1"** — listing grievances (ICE raids, Medicaid cuts, union busting) and calling for "labor, environmental, and social movement coalitions." The call is a pressure campaign designed to build the organization's profile, not a concrete revolutionary strategy. It does not address who would lead such a strike, how it would bypass the union bureaucracies that have systematically suppressed rank-and-file militancy, or what political program it would advance. The DSA/SAlt tendency has repeatedly used "strike" rhetoric to channel worker anger into electoral campaigns for the Democratic Party.
Sources Socialist Alternative State of the Union: Trump insists we're in the 'Golden Age of America' · Socialist Alternative The case for a nationwide general strike on May 1
### SWP UK (Socialist Worker) The SWP celebrated the **Gorton and Denton by-election result** as a defeat for Reform UK rather than a defeat for Labour, framing the Green win primarily as an "anti-racist" victory. This is a classic identity-politics framing that avoids the class question entirely. The Green Party is a bourgeois parliamentary party that cannot represent a genuine socialist alternative to Labour; the SWP's celebration reflects its adaptation to left-liberal politics. SWP also reported on **record-high zero-hours contracts** in the UK — correctly identifying "casualisation" and "exploitation" under the Labour government, but proposing only a general pressure campaign on Labour as the solution. No independent political program for the working class is offered.
Sources Socialist Worker Anti-racists celebrate as Reform UK trashed in Gorton and Denton · Socialist Worker Use of zero hours contracts reaches record high
### Socialist Appeal / RCP (IMT) The IMT published on the **Chagos Islands deal** collapsing as a political crisis for Starmer, framing it primarily as a parliamentary "blow" rather than analyzing the anti-colonial implications or the role of US-UK imperialism in maintaining Indian Ocean military bases. Separately, the RCP published **organizational self-promotion** reporting recruitment figures — "400 members and rising" at their London Marxist School — with no coverage of actual UK workers' struggles in this period. The IMT tendency combines formal Marxist phraseology with organizational ambition and avoidance of programmatic confrontation with reformism.
Sources Communist.red Chagos Islands deal blows up in Starmer's face · Communist.red Revolutionary Communist Party on the advance
